signals: stepwise approach to spirometry allows for ease and reliability of interpretation
FEV1/FVC ratio and FVC are used together to identify obstructive defects
A low FVC is defined as a value below the 5th percentile
Obstructive defects should be assessed for reversibility
proper use of pulmonary function tests can help differentiate many of the causes of dyspnea

extraction.summary: Spirometry is used to diagnose and monitor obstructive and restrictive lung diseases including asthma and COPD. A stepwise approach evaluates FEV1/FVC ratio for obstruction, FVC for restriction, and bronchodilator response for reversibility.

population.age_criteria: Adults and children/adolescents 5-18 years of age
population.risk_criteria: Symptomatic patients with suspected COPD or asthma; patients at risk of pulmonary disease
population.disease_definition: Suspected respiratory symptoms, abnormal laboratory tests, preoperative assessment, monitoring known pulmonary disease
population.severity_staging: Mild (>70% predicted), Moderate (60-69%), Moderately severe (50-59%), Severe (35-49%), Very severe (<35%)

key_thresholds.context: Low FVC in adults (lower limit of normal)
Significant reversibility in adults (FEV1 or FVC)

recommendations.recommendation: Use spirometry as part of diagnostic workup in patients with presumed COPD or asthma
recommendations.notes: Recommended by ATS/ERS, GOLD, Global Initiative for Asthma, and AAAAI

recommendations.recommendation: Do not screen asymptomatic adults for COPD with spirometry
recommendations.notes: USPSTF, AAFP, ATS/ERS, and GOLD recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Withhold bronchodilators before testing: SABA 4-6 hours, SAMA 12 hours, LABA 24 hours, LAMA 36-48 hours
recommendations.notes: If diagnosing underlying disorder or assessing bronchodilator responsiveness

recommendations.recommendation: Perform full pulmonary function testing for restrictive patterns and mixed patterns if FVC doesn't improve with bronchodilator
Consider bronchoprovocation testing if spirometry normal but exercise/allergen-induced asthma suspected
For FEV1 <70% predicted, may try therapeutic bronchodilator trial instead of bronchoprovocation testing
Don't diagnose or manage asthma without spirometry
recommendations.notes: American Academy of Allergy, Asthma and Immunology Choosing Wisely recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Use standardized spirometry for COPD and asthma diagnosis
Consider bronchoprovocation testing for asthma diagnosis when spirometry is normal
recommendations.notes: Methacholine or mannitol challenge testing per ERS standards

recommendations.recommendation: Use post-bronchodilator values for COPD diagnosis
recommendations.notes: Post-dilator FEV1/FVC ratio more accurate than pre-dilator values

medications.dose: Increasing concentrations up to 4 mg/mL cumulative
Increasing concentrations up to 635 mg cumulative

red_flags: Poor quality spirometry results affecting interpretation
Mixed pattern not improving with bronchodilator
Invalid spirometry test quality criteria not met
Severe airway obstruction with acute symptoms
Unable to perform adequate spirometry maneuvers
extraction.follow_up: FVC and FEV1 values decrease by 20-30 mL per year normally. Review previous spirometry results to assess disease progression or treatment response. Variations in test quality may affect comparisons.
extraction.escalation_path: Refer for full pulmonary function testing: 1) Restrictive pattern on spirometry, 2) Mixed pattern if FVC does not improve significantly after bronchodilator administration. Consider bronchoprovocation testing if spirometry normal but clinical suspicion for exercise/allergen-induced asthma.
